title: Assignment problem (min cost matching) (Hungarian / min-cost flow).
description: Assignment problem (min cost matching) (Hungarian / min-cost flow).

examples.explanation: Permutations of [0,1]: [0,1]: cost=5+6=11; [1,0]: cost=8+3=11. Both give 11, first assignment [0,1] selected.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n! * n)
approaches.summary: Generate all permutations of assignments between n workers and n jobs, compute the total cost for each permutation, and select the minimum. This is straightforward but impractical for large n due to factorial growth.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Assignment Problem
ladders.desc: Complete solution: generate all permutations of assignments between n workers and n jobs, compute total cost for each, and select minimum.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the assignment problem by exhaustively checking every possible assignment between workers and jobs. Intuition: Since each worker must be assigned to exactly one job and each job to exactly one worker, assignments correspond to permutations of job indices assigned to workers in order. By generating all n! permutations, we guarantee checking the optimal assignment. Step-by-step: 1) Generate all permutations of [0,1,...,n-1] representing job assignments for workers 0 to n-1. 2) For each permutation, compute total cost by summing cost[worker][job] for each worker-job pair. 3) Track minimum cost and corresponding assignment. Time: O(n! * n) because n! permutations each requiring O(n) cost calculation. Space: O(n) for storing current permutation and best assignment. Connection to L1: This uses permutation generation (L1) as the key subroutine. Common mistakes: Not handling n=0 or n=1 correctly, inefficient permutation generation, or incorrect cost indexing. Real code pattern: Use itertools.permutations in Python or recursive backtracking.

examples.trace: Permutations of [0,1]: [0,1]: cost=5+6=11; [1,0]: cost=8+3=11. Both give 11, first assignment [0,1] selected.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate All Permutations
ladders.desc: Generate all permutations of a list of n elements.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine generates all possible orderings (permutations) of n distinct elements, which is essential for brute force assignment. Intuition: Each permutation represents a unique assignment of jobs to workers. By systematically exploring all n! orderings, we ensure no assignment is missed. Step-by-step using backtracking: 1) Start with an empty current permutation and list of unused elements. 2) Recursively: pick an unused element, add to current permutation, mark as used, recurse. 3) When current permutation has n elements, record it. 4) Backtrack by removing last element and marking it unused. Time: O(n! * n) to generate n! permutations each of length n. Space: O(n) for recursion stack and current permutation. Connection to L0: This generates the assignments checked in L0. Common mistakes: Not handling duplicates correctly (not needed here as elements are distinct indices), inefficient copying of permutations. Real code pattern: Use recursion with a visited array or swap-based algorithm.

examples.trace: Start: perm=[], unused={0,1,2}. Pick 0: perm=[0], unused={1,2}. Pick 1: perm=[0,1], unused={2}. Pick 2: perm=[0,1,2] -> record. Backtrack: perm=[0,1], unused={2}. Backtrack: perm=[0], unused={1,2}. Pick 2: perm=[0,2], unused={1}. Pick 1: perm=[0,2,1] -> record. Continue similarly for other branches.

Start: perm=[], unused={'A','B'}. Pick 'A': perm=['A'], unused={'B'}. Pick 'B': perm=['A','B'] -> record. Backtrack: perm=['A'], unused={'B'}. Backtrack: perm=[], unused={'A','B'}. Pick 'B': perm=['B'], unused={'A'}. Pick 'A': perm=['B','A'] -> record.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Compute Total Cost for an Assignment
ladders.desc: Given an assignment permutation, compute total cost by summing worker-job costs.
ladders.explanation: This core logic calculates the total cost for a specific assignment, which is the heart of evaluating each permutation in brute force. Intuition: The assignment is represented as a permutation where index i is worker i and value perm[i] is the job assigned to that worker. Total cost is the sum of individual costs from the cost matrix. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize total=0. 2) For each worker i from 0 to n-1: total += cost[i][perm[i]]. 3) Return total. Time: O(n) for iterating through n workers. Space: O(1) aside from input. Connection to L0: This computation is done for each permutation in L0. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in indexing, misunderstanding permutation representation, not handling empty case. Real code pattern: Simple loop over indices.

examples.trace: Worker 0: job=0, cost=1. Worker 1: job=2, cost=1. Worker 2: job=1, cost=1. Sum: 1+1+1=3.
Worker 0: job=1, cost=8. Worker 1: job=0, cost=3. Sum: 8+3=11.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate Over List Indices
ladders.desc: Loop through indices of a list to access elements.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves iterating through the indices of a list, which is fundamental for accessing elements in sequences like cost matrices or permutations. Intuition: In programming, lists are indexed starting from 0, and looping through indices allows systematic access to each element. Step-by-step: For a list of length n, use a loop (e.g., for i in range(n)) to generate indices 0,1,...,n-1. Within the loop, access list[i] to get the element. Time: O(n) for n iterations. Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This iteration is used in L2 to sum costs for each worker. Common mistakes: Using 1-based indexing incorrectly, iterating beyond list length, or modifying list while iterating. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(list)): ...

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access 2D List Element
ladders.desc: Access an element in a 2D list using row and column indices.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves accessing a specific element in a two-dimensional list (matrix), which is crucial for retrieving costs in the assignment problem. Intuition: A 2D list is a list of lists, where the first index selects the row (outer list) and the second index selects the column (element within that row). Step-by-step: Given cost matrix cost, row index i, and column index j, access cost[i][j]. Ensure indices are within bounds: 0 <= i < len(cost) and 0 <= j < len(cost[i]). Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This operation is used in L2 to get cost for each worker-job pair. Common mistakes: Index out of range, confusing row and column order, or assuming rectangular matrix without validation. Real code pattern: value = matrix[i][j].

examples.trace: cost[0] is [1,2,3]. cost[0][2] is the third element: 3.
cost[1] is [30,40]. cost[1][0] is the first element: 30.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — List and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understand lists as ordered collections and access elements by index.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces lists, which are ordered collections of items, and indexing, which allows accessing specific elements by their position. Intuition: A list is like a sequence of boxes numbered starting from 0; indexing lets you look inside a specific box. Step-by-step: 1) Define a list, e.g., my_list = [10, 20, 30]. 2) Access elements: my_list[0] gives 10 (first element), my_list[1] gives 20, etc. 3) Indices must be integers and within range 0 to len(my_list)-1. Time: O(1) for access. Space: O(n) to store list. Connection to L4: This basic list knowledge is required for accessing elements in 2D lists. Common mistakes: Using index -1 without understanding (last element), index out of bounds, or confusing lists with other data structures. Real code pattern: element = list[index].

examples.trace: List has elements at positions: 0->5, 1->10, 2->15. Index 1 accesses the second element: 10.
List has elements at positions: 0->'apple', 1->'banana', 2->'cherry'. Index 0 accesses the first element: 'apple'.

approaches.name: Hungarian Algorithm O(n³)
approaches.summary: Use the Hungarian algorithm, which reduces the cost matrix through row and column operations to find an optimal assignment. It efficiently solves the assignment problem by iteratively improving a feasible solution.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Hungarian Algorithm for Minimum Cost Assignment
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the Hungarian algorithm to solve the assignment problem with O(n³) time complexity.
ladders.explanation: The Hungarian algorithm solves the assignment problem by transforming the cost matrix through row and column operations to find an optimal matching. The intuition is that subtracting constants from rows and columns doesn't change the optimal assignment, allowing us to create a matrix with zeros where assignments can be made. The algorithm works in phases: 1) Reduce the matrix by subtracting row minima and column minima. 2) Find a maximum matching of zeros using König's theorem. 3) If the matching covers all rows, we have an optimal solution. 4) Otherwise, adjust the matrix using the minimum uncovered value and repeat. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Subtract the smallest element in each row from all elements in that row. 2) Subtract the smallest element in each column from all elements in that column. 3) Cover all zeros with minimum number of lines (rows+columns). 4) If number of lines equals n, optimal assignment exists at zero positions. 5) Otherwise, find smallest uncovered value, subtract it from all uncovered elements, and add it to elements covered twice. 6) Repeat from step 3. Time complexity is O(n³) due to the iterative covering process. Space complexity is O(n²) for storing the cost matrix and assignments. This connects to L1 by using the zero covering subroutine as its core component. Common mistakes include incorrect line covering logic and forgetting to handle the adjustment step properly. Real code pattern involves maintaining matrices for costs, row/column assignments, and line covering states.

examples.trace: Step 1: Original matrix [[3,5],[10,1]]. Step 2: Subtract row minima: row0 min=3 → [[0,2],[9,0]]. Step 3: Subtract column minima: col0 min=0, col1 min=0 → matrix unchanged. Step 4: Cover zeros: line through row0 covers both zeros. Need 2 lines but have 1. Step 5: Smallest uncovered value = 2. Subtract from uncovered: [[0,0],[7,0]]. Add to double-covered: none. Step 6: Cover zeros: line through col0 and col1 covers all zeros. 2 lines = n=2. Step 7: Find assignment: row0-col1 (0), row1-col0 (7) or row0-col0 (0), row1-col1 (0). Choose row0-col0 and row1-col1 with original costs 3+1=4.

Step 1: [[2,7,5],[3,6,1],[8,4,9]]. Step 2: Row minima: 2,1,4 → [[0,5,3],[2,5,0],[4,0,5]]. Step 3: Column minima: 0,0,0 → unchanged. Step 4: Cover zeros: lines through row1 and col1 cover all zeros. 2 lines < n=3. Step 5: Smallest uncovered = 2. Adjust: [[0,3,1],[4,5,0],[4,0,3]]. Step 6: Cover: lines through row0, row1, col1 cover all zeros. 3 lines = n. Step 7: Assignment: row0-col0 (0), row1-col2 (0), row2-col1 (0). Original costs: 2+1+4=7.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Cover Zeros with Minimum Lines
ladders.desc: Implementation of König's theorem to cover all zeros in a matrix with minimum lines.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine finds the minimum number of rows and columns needed to cover all zero elements in a matrix, which is crucial for determining if an optimal assignment exists. The intuition is based on bipartite matching: zeros represent potential assignments, and covering them with lines corresponds to finding a vertex cover in the bipartite graph. The algorithm uses a greedy approach with DFS: 1) Try to match each row to an uncovered zero column. 2) Mark matched columns. 3) For unmatched rows, mark them and all columns connected through alternating paths. 4) Cover all unmarked rows and marked columns. Step-by-step: Initialize row_cover[n]=False, col_cover[n]=False. For each row i: if not row_cover[i], DFS to find augmenting path. After DFS, mark all visited rows and columns. The lines are unmarked rows + marked columns. Time complexity is O(n³) in worst case due to DFS on n rows. Space complexity is O(n²) for the matrix and O(n) for cover arrays. This feeds into L0 by providing the covering check needed for the Hungarian algorithm's main loop. Common mistakes include incorrect DFS implementation and misunderstanding the covering rule. Real code pattern involves recursion or stack-based DFS to find augmenting paths.

examples.trace: Step 1: Matrix zeros at (0,0),(0,2),(1,1),(2,0),(2,2). Step 2: Try matching: row0→col0 matched. row1→col1 matched. row2→col0 (taken), try alternating: row0→col2, row2→col0 matched. Step 3: Unmatched rows: none. Mark: start from unmatched rows (none). Step 4: Cover: unmarked rows (none) + marked columns (none) = 0 lines initially. Actually need lines: row0 covers (0,0),(0,2); col1 covers (1,1); total 2 lines cover all zeros.

Step 1: Zeros at (0,1),(0,2),(1,0),(1,2),(2,0),(2,1). Step 2: Matching: row0→col1, row1→col0, row2→? col2 available? Actually (2,2)=1 not zero. Try: row0→col1, row1→col0, row2 has zeros at (2,0) taken, (2,1) free → match row2→col1 conflict, alternating path row0→col2, row2→col1 matched. Step 3: All rows matched. Mark: no unmatched rows. Step 4: Cover: need lines through all rows to cover all zeros since zeros are scattered.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Matrix Reduction for Zero Creation
ladders.desc: Reduce cost matrix by subtracting row and column minima to create zeros for potential assignments.
ladders.explanation: This is the core technique that enables the Hungarian algorithm to work. The intuition is that subtracting a constant from a row or column changes all assignment costs equally for that row/column, so the relative differences between assignments remain the same, and the optimal assignment doesn't change. By creating zeros through these operations, we identify potential zero-cost assignments. The algorithm: 1) For each row, find the minimum value and subtract it from all elements in that row. 2) For each column, find the minimum value and subtract it from all elements in that column. This ensures at least one zero in each row and column. Time complexity is O(n²) for scanning the matrix twice. Space complexity is O(n²) for the matrix. This feeds into L1 by providing the zero matrix needed for line covering. Common mistakes include modifying the original matrix incorrectly or not handling negative values properly. Real code pattern involves simple nested loops for row and column operations.

examples.trace: Step 1: Row minima: row0 min=3, row1 min=1. Step 2: Subtract: row0: [3-3,5-3]=[0,2]; row1: [10-1,1-1]=[9,0]. Step 3: Column minima: col0 min=0, col1 min=0. Step 4: Subtract: col0: [0-0,9-0]=[0,9]; col1: [2-0,0-0]=[2,0]. Result: [[0,2],[9,0]].

Step 1: Row minima: 2,1,4. Step 2: Subtract: row0: [0,5,3]; row1: [2,5,0]; row2: [4,0,5]. Step 3: Column minima: col0 min=0, col1 min=0, col2 min=0. Step 4: Subtract: unchanged. Result: [[0,5,3],[2,5,0],[4,0,5]].

ladders.title: Building Block — Find Minimum Value in Array
ladders.desc: Identify the smallest element in a row or column of a matrix.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is fundamental because the Hungarian algorithm repeatedly needs to find minima for row and column operations. The intuition is simple: scanning through elements to find the smallest value. The algorithm: Initialize min_val = INF. For each element in the row/column, if current element < min_val, update min_val. Time complexity is O(n) for a row/column of length n. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the minima needed for matrix reduction. Common mistakes include incorrect initialization (e.g., using 0 instead of first element) or not handling empty arrays. Real code pattern is a simple loop with a comparison.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize min=3 (first element). Step 2: Compare 5>3, min stays 3. Step 3: Compare 2<3, min=2. Step 4: Compare 8>2, min stays 2. Result: 2.
Step 1: Elements: 4,1,7,3. Step 2: Initialize min=4. Step 3: Compare 1<4, min=1. Step 4: Compare 7>1, min stays 1. Step 5: Compare 3>1, min stays 1. Result: 1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Subtract Constant from Array
ladders.desc: Subtract a given value from all elements in a row or column.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is used repeatedly in the Hungarian algorithm to adjust the cost matrix. The intuition is arithmetic: subtracting the same value from all elements in a row/column shifts them equally, preserving relative differences. The algorithm: For each element in the array, new_value = old_value - constant. Time complexity is O(n) for an array of length n. Space complexity is O(1) if done in-place, O(n) if new array created. This feeds into L3 by being the action taken after finding the minimum value. Common mistakes include incorrect indexing or modifying wrong elements. Real code pattern is a simple loop with subtraction.

examples.trace: Step 1: Element 0: 3-2=1. Step 2: Element 1: 5-2=3. Step 3: Element 2: 7-2=5. Result: [1,3,5].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Matrix?
ladders.desc: Understand a 2D array (matrix) as a grid of numbers with rows and columns.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: a matrix is a rectangular array of numbers arranged in rows and columns. The intuition is like a spreadsheet or grid where each cell has a value. Key concepts: 1) Dimensions: m rows × n columns. 2) Indexing: element at row i, column j is M[i][j]. 3) Row: all elements in a horizontal line. 4) Column: all elements in a vertical line. For the assignment problem, the matrix represents costs: row i is a worker, column j is a job, M[i][j] is cost of assigning worker i to job j. Time/space: Accessing an element is O(1), storing is O(mn). This feeds into L4 by providing the structure on which operations are performed. Common mistakes include confusing rows and columns or incorrect indexing. Real code pattern in Python: matrix = [[1,2],[3,4]] where matrix[0][1] = 2.

examples.output: 2 rows, 2 columns; element at row0 col1 is 2
examples.trace: Step 1: Visualize: Row 0: [1,2]. Row 1: [3,4]. Step 2: Columns: Col 0: [1,3]. Col 1: [2,4]. Step 3: Access: matrix[0][0]=1, matrix[0][1]=2, matrix[1][0]=3, matrix[1][1]=4.
Step 1: Dimensions: 2 rows (0,1), 3 columns (0,1,2). Step 2: Row 0: [5,6,7]. Row 1: [8,9,10]. Step 3: Column 0: [5,8]. Column 1: [6,9]. Column 2: [7,10].
